topic: Geographic Perspectives: Global Change (Population Distribution, Global Climate, Global Resource Consumption)
subject: geography

items.q: Is the world's population spread fairly evenly across the land?
items.correct: No - distribution is highly uneven: huge concentrations occur in South and East Asia and in coastal lowlands, while deserts, high mountains and polar areas are sparsely populated.
items.misconception: People are spread roughly evenly, so a country's population just reflects its size.
items.random_wrong: Most of the world's people live in the polar regions.
items.why: Physical factors (climate, relief, water) and economic history cluster people dramatically; about two-thirds of humanity lives within a few hundred kilometres of a coast.

items.q: Do the countries that emit the most greenhouse gases suffer the most from climate change?
items.correct: Not necessarily - many low-income countries emit little but are highly vulnerable because of exposure and low adaptive capacity, while high emitters often have more resources to adapt.
items.misconception: Whoever causes the most emissions automatically experiences the worst impacts.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only affects countries on the Equator.
items.why: This equity gap between responsibility and vulnerability is central to climate justice debates in the IB course.

items.q: Does ecological overshoot mean Earth's resources are already used up?
items.correct: No - it means humanity's annual demand exceeds the planet's annual biocapacity, so we are drawing down natural capital, which is unsustainable over time.
items.misconception: Overshoot means resources are completely exhausted right now.
items.random_wrong: Ecological footprint measures the size of a person's shoes.
items.why: Overshoot is about flows versus regeneration rates, like spending more than your income while savings still remain.

items.q: A megacity is an urban area with more than how many people?
items.why: It's 10 million. “1 million” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which factor best explains low population density in the Sahara?
items.correct: A hot arid climate limits water supply and farming
items.misconception: It is too mountainous for any settlement
items.random_wrong: Governments ban people from living there
items.why: Correct answer: A hot arid climate limits water supply and farming. “It is too mountainous for any settlement” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Climate change vulnerability depends on exposure, sensitivity and what else?
items.why: Correct answer: Adaptive capacity. “Latitude only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The interdependence of water, food and energy resources
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The interdependence of water, food and energy resources. “A global trade agreement” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In 2022 the UN announced the world population had reached how many billion?
items.why: Exact answer: 8 billion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
